title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: The panel (kiss_zealot, perf_skeptic, quality_critic — no security_hawk findings were present in the pool) raised 44 raw items; I collapsed them to 26. Every claim was verified against the diff and all held up, so nothing was dropped as a false positive — this PR genuinely ships a wall of must-fix security defects. Heavy merging was required because kiss_zealot and quality_critic independently flagged the same 11 critical issues (committed secrets, two auth backdoors, two RCE-via-eval sinks, MD5 hashing, PAN/CVC storage+logging, full-PAN CSV export, plaintext-password logging, password_hash leak, reset-token leak) — those are the high-confidence set. The cross-tenant webhook fan-out and webhook SSRF were each raised by 2-3 personas and kept. I folded the two standalone `@ts-nocheck` complaints into their root-cause eval/new Function findings, and merged kiss_zealot's two module-global complaints into one. Remaining single-persona items (CORS, sync fan-out latency, body-limit DoS, missing requireAdmin mount, login enumeration, non-expiring JWT, console.log usage, weak tests, and several in-memory scalability notes) were validated and kept at honest severities.

findings.title: Live production secrets committed; .gitignore patched with !.env to force it
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY (prefixed pg_live_) are committed in .env, and .gitignore was deliberately changed to `!.env` (removing the `.env` rule) to keep them tracked. All three are now permanently in git history. Rotate all three credentials immediately, revert the `!.env` override, delete .env from the repo, and inject secrets via env vars / a secrets manager. A .env.example with placeholders is fine to commit; the real file never is.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 grants full admin identity to any unauthenticated caller
findings.message: Any request appending ?adminBypass=1 gets req.user set to { userId: 'bypass', email: 'admin@admin.com', role: 'admin' } with no token, secret, IP restriction, or logging — a permanent unauthenticated admin backdoor on every route. The adminController re-checks `req.query.adminBypass !== '1'` inline too, so it is wired in two places. Delete the block (and the inline controller checks). If a staging shortcut is truly needed, gate it behind an env-only secret header and log every invocation.

findings.title: Hardcoded 'admin-master-key' bypasses JWT verification entirely
findings.message: verifyToken short-circuits to a full admin session for the literal string 'admin-master-key', bypassing all signature verification. The string is in git history and visible to anyone with repo read access, and cannot be revoked without a code change + deploy. Delete this branch completely — there must be no secret-independent bypass inside the verifier.

findings.title: eval() on user-supplied WHERE clauses — server-side RCE
findings.message: evalWhere() calls eval(expr) on a clause string, and listAllUsers(filter) passes req.query.filter straight through with no sanitisation, so e.g. ?filter=role=='user'||process.exit(1) executes arbitrary Node in-process. The `@ts-nocheck` at the top of the file is the compiler warning you the FakeDb pseudo-SQL engine shouldn't exist. Delete FakeDb entirely and replace with a plain UserRow[] using .find()/.filter() and typed predicates — no eval, no SQL parser, no template-literal query construction.

findings.title: new Function(filterExpr) executes arbitrary JS from the ?filter query param
findings.message: new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')') is eval() by another name; filterExpr arrives from req.query.filter with no validation. Any authenticated admin — or anyone using ?adminBypass=1 — runs arbitrary Node in the server process. The `@ts-nocheck` on this file exists because this untyped dynamic execution can't be typed. Replace with a finite typed filter schema (zod enum of allowed fields + operators + scalar values).

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing — no salt, no work factor
findings.message: createHash('md5') is a general-purpose digest with no per-user salt and sub-millisecond throughput; a leaked store is reversible via rainbow tables. Use bcrypt (cost >= 12) or argon2id, and make verifyPassword use a constant-time comparison. Existing MD5-stored passwords must be invalidated with a forced reset.

findings.title: Full card_number and cvc stored on transaction and console.log'd in plaintext
findings.message: Lines 44-45 attach card_number and cvc to the persisted Transaction, then line 52 does console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction)), dumping both to stdout and bypassing pino's redaction. PCI DSS forbids storing CVC after auth and forbids writing PANs to logs. Remove card_number and cvc from the type and object literal (keep only cardLast4), delete the console.log, and audit/purge any records already persisted with these fields.

findings.title: CSV export includes full card_number column — PCI DSS violation
findings.message: The header on line 49 includes 'card_number' and tx.card_number is emitted per row, exporting raw PANs in a plaintext file any admin (or bypass caller) can download. Remove the column entirely; card_last4 is already in the export and is the correct substitute.

findings.title: console.log(req.body) writes the plaintext password to stdout on every registration
findings.message: console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body)) serialises the password before hashing into every log sink, bypassing pino's redaction. Delete this line; if tracing is needed log only request id + email at debug level.

findings.title: password_hash returned in the register 201 response
findings.message: The register response includes password_hash. Since it's an unsalted MD5, the hash is effectively the password and enables offline cracking. Remove it — the contract should be { token, user: { id, email, role } }.

findings.title: Password-reset token returned in HTTP response (and logged) instead of emailed
findings.message: requestPasswordReset both console.log's the reset token and returns it as { ok: true, resetToken }. The token is a bearer credential; returning it to the (unauthenticated) caller means anyone who knows a victim's email can reset their account in one call. Deliver it only via email, log nothing, and always respond { ok: true }.

findings.title: fireForTransaction delivers every transaction to every webhook — cross-tenant data leak
findings.message: The loop iterates the global `webhooks` array with no owner filter, so every registered webhook from every account receives every charge — leaking payment data across tenants. Filter by the charging account's ownerId (or key the store by ownerId) before firing. Also add a per-request fetch timeout so one hung subscriber can't stall the loop.

findings.title: No URL validation on webhook registration — SSRF via fetch with redirect:follow
findings.message: register(url) accepts any string and fireForTransaction calls fetch(hook.url, { redirect: 'follow' }) unconditionally. A user can register http://169.254.169.254/... (cloud IMDS), an internal hostname, or an RFC-1918 address and have the server POST transaction payloads there. Validate at registration time that the URL is HTTPS with a publicly routable host (reject loopback/link-local/RFC-1918), and set redirect to 'error' to block redirect-chain bypasses.

findings.title: cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true }) is spec-invalid and insecure
findings.message: The CORS spec forbids credentials: true with a wildcard origin — browsers reject it, so this breaks the frontend it was meant to enable while also being insecure in intent. Enumerate allowed origins (e.g. ['https://app.honeybook.com']) or drop credentials for genuinely public endpoints.

findings.title: Synchronous webhook fan-out blocks the charge response
findings.message: await this.webhooks.fireForTransaction(transaction) runs inline before the charge returns, so charge latency becomes gateway latency + the sum of every webhook's latency, serially and with no timeout. One slow/hung subscriber stalls the payment. Move delivery off the request path: enqueue { transactionId } and return immediately.

findings.title: JSON body limit raised from 64kb to 5mb globally (80x) — DoS amplifier
findings.message: express.json({ limit: '5mb' }) applies to every route including /charge and /auth/*, letting an attacker force 5mb of JSON parsing per request cheaply. If admin export/payloads need a larger body, scope a 5mb express.json only to /admin/* and leave all other routes at 64kb.

findings.title: Admin authz check copy-pasted into 4 handlers; requireAdmin middleware never mounted
findings.message: The email.endsWith('@admin.com')||'@honeybook.com'||adminBypass check is duplicated verbatim in listUsers, listTransactions, stats, and exportCsv. A requireAdmin middleware already exists in authMiddleware.ts for exactly this but is never mounted. Mount it (router.get('/admin/users', authMiddleware, requireAdmin, ...)) and delete all four inline copies so the domain list lives in one place. (Drop the adminBypass branch as part of the backdoor fix above.)

findings.title: Login returns 404 for unknown email — user enumeration + wrong status
findings.message: Returning 404 'no user with that email' reveals which emails are registered. Unknown-email and wrong-password must both return an identical 401 with a generic 'invalid credentials' message. 404 is also semantically wrong — the endpoint exists, only the credential is invalid.

findings.title: signToken issues non-expiring JWTs
findings.message: jwt.sign(payload, SECRET) with no options creates tokens valid forever, so a stolen token is a permanent credential. Add { expiresIn: '24h' } (shorter for sensitive ops) and document a refresh strategy.

findings.title: console.log used across new files instead of the pino logger
findings.message: Webhook lifecycle events (lines 31/41/43) use console.log, as do userStore.findUserByEmail (logs the SQL string containing the email) and authController. console.* bypasses pino's levels, structured fields, and redaction. Replace with the injected logger using structured fields ({ webhookId, txId, status }). (The password and reset-token logs are covered by their own must findings.)

findings.title: Auth tests only assert token toBeDefined() — pass even for a bogus token
findings.message: Both cases stop at expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined(), which passes for an empty string or any truthy value and never verifies the token decodes to the right userId/email/role. Missing: duplicate-email -> 409, wrong password -> 401, missing field -> 400, the /auth/me round-trip, and admin/webhook coverage. For auth code the negative paths and bypass attempts are the cases that matter most.

findings.title: exportTransactionsCsv buffers the entire transaction table in memory
findings.message: findAll() loads every row and rows.map(...) builds one in-memory string before sending. Memory grows linearly with row count and there's no streaming/pagination, risking heap spikes/OOM at scale. When backed by a real DB, stream rows in fixed batches and res.write() each batch.

findings.title: stats() issues two unbounded full-table reads per call
findings.message: findAll() and listAllUsers() are full scans on every /admin/stats hit. Once backed by a real DB, compute aggregates with a single GROUP BY query (or maintain incremental counters) rather than pulling the full dataset into the process per read.

findings.title: No pagination on /admin/users and /admin/transactions
findings.message: Neither handler reads limit/offset/cursor; both return the full table. Add a mandatory bounded page size (e.g. limit = Math.min(req.query.limit ?? 50, 500)) in the controllers and matching support in AdminService.listUsers / listAllTransactions.

findings.title: stats() returns snake_case keys in an otherwise camelCase API
findings.message: users_count, transactions_count, total_volume are snake_case while the rest of the API uses camelCase (cardLast4, createdAt). Rename to usersCount, transactionsCount, totalVolume so consumers don't special-case this endpoint.

findings.title: Module-level mutable singletons (db + webhooks) leak state between tests
findings.message: const db = new FakeDb() (userStore.ts:70) and const webhooks: Webhook[] = [] (webhooksService.ts:11) are module-level singletons shared across all instances and tests, so a user/webhook created in one test is visible in the next. The codebase already injects repositories — do the same here so state can be constructed and reset per test.
